definitions.definition: treating someone in a way that is right or reasonable, or treating a group of people equally and not allowing personal opinions to influence your judgment
examples: a fair trial
Why should I have to do all the cleaning? It's not fair!
It's not fair on Joe (= it is not right) to make him do all the work!
It's not fair that she's allowed to go and I'm not!
It's not fair to blame me for everything!
She's scrupulously fair with all her employees (= she treats them all equally).
She claims her article was a fair comment on (= a reasonable thing to say about) a matter of public interest.
He offered to do all the cleaning if I did all the cooking, which seemed like a fair (= reasonable) deal.

definitions.definition: If something is fair, it is reasonable and is what you expect or deserve
examples: I thought it was a fair price that she was offering.
I'm willing to do my fair (= equal) share of the work.
All the workers want is a fair wage for the work that they do.

definitions.definition: If a game or competition is fair, it is done according to the rules
examples: It was a fair fight.

definitions.definition: it is the right way to treat someone and what they deserve
examples: I think it's only fair to tell you that we have had over 300 applications for this job.

definitions.definition: it is true to say
examples: I think it's fair to say (that) you've done less of the work than I have.

definitions.definition: considering everything that has an effect on a situation, so that a fair judgment can be made
examples: He's done the job badly but, to be fair, I gave him very little time to do it.

definitions.definition: something you say to show that you understand why someone has done or said something
examples: "I'm just annoyed with him because he's behaved so badly." "Fair enough."

definitions.definition: something that you say when you want someone to behave reasonably or treat you the same as other people
examples: Come on, it's my turn. Fair's fair!

definitions.definition: an opportunity to explain something or give your opinions, without other people trying to influence the situation
examples: He didn't think that he got a fair hearing in court.

definitions.definition: in an honest way and without any doubt
examples: We won the match fair and square.

definitions.definition: If you hit someone fair and square on a particular part of their body, you hit that person hard, exactly on that part
examples: He hit me fair and square on the nose.

definitions.definition: (of skin) pale, or (of hair) pale yellow or gold
examples: She's got fair hair and blue eyes.
a fair complexion
My sister has dark hair but my brother's fair (= he has fair hair).
He's fair-haired.
All my family are fair-skinned.

definitions.definition: quite large
examples: We've had a fair amount of rain this week.
We've had a fair number of applicants.
It's a fair-sized garden.
We've come a long way, but there's still a fair way (= quite a long distance) to go.

definitions.definition: neither very good nor very bad
examples: Films are rated on a scale of poor, fair, good and excellent.
I was fair at science but it was never my thing.

definitions.definition: (of an idea, guess, or chance) good, but not excellent
examples: I think I've got a fair idea of (= I understand reasonably well) what you want.
She's got a fair chance of winning (= there is a reasonable chance that she will win).

definitions.definition: (of weather) pleasant and dry
examples: Fair weather was forecast for the following day.

definitions.definition: (of a woman) beautiful
examples: a fair maiden

definitions.definition: If something is fair, it is reasonable and is what you expect or deserve
examples: I thought it was a fair price that she was offering.

definitions.definition: If a game or competition is fair, it is done according to the rules

definitions.definition: it is the right way to treat someone and what they deserve
examples: I think it's only fair to tell you that we have had over 300 applications for this job.

definitions.definition: it is true to say
examples: I think it's fair to say (that) you've done less of the work than I have.

definitions.definition: considering everything that has an effect on a situation, so that a fair judgment can be made
examples: He's done the job badly but, to be fair, I gave him very little time to do it.

definitions.definition: something you say to show that you understand why someone has done or said something
examples: "I'm just annoyed with him because he's behaved so badly." "Fair enough."

definitions.definition: something that you say when you want someone to behave reasonably or treat you the same as other people

definitions.definition: an opportunity to explain something or give your opinions, without other people trying to influence the situation

definitions.definition: in an honest way and without any doubt

definitions.definition: If you hit someone fair and square on a particular part of their body, you hit that person hard, exactly on that part

definitions.definition: neither very good nor very bad

definitions.definition: (of an idea, guess, or chance) good, but not excellent

definitions.definition: to become lower in size, amount, or strength

definitions.definition: to belong to a particular group, subject, or area

definitions.definition: to be beaten or defeated

definitions.definition: If soldiers fall while fighting, they are killed

to use physical force to try to defeat another person or group of people
examples: There were children fighting in the playground.

definitions.definition: to use a lot of effort to defeat or achieve something, or to stop something happening
examples: He fought the disease bravely for three years.
We need the public's help in fighting crime.
He fought against racism.
Vitamin C is thought to help fight colds and flu.

The bank fought off (= successfully prevented) a takeover by another bank recently.
I was getting a cold at the start of the week but I seem to have fought it off (= got rid of it).

definitions.definition: to argue
examples: I wish they wouldn't fight in front of the kids.

definitions.definition: to stop rising, falling, or changing so quickly, or to make the rate of change slower
examples: Television ad rates have flattened, if not declined slightly.

definitions.definition: to make the rate of change slower for something that might be shown as a line on a graph, especially to make the increase in the rate of infections slower when a disease is spreading
examples: These public health measures are designed to flatten the curve in the spread of the disease.

Preventing a health care system from being overwhelmed in an epidemic requires a society to "flatten the curve" - that is, slow the rate of infection so there aren't too many cases that need hospitalization at one time.
This sort of banking lowers long-term interest rates; it flattens the curve.

definitions.definition: If you flip something, you turn it over quickly one or more times, and if something flips, it turns over quickly
examples: I flipped the book (over) to look at the back cover.
I lost my place in my book when the pages flipped over in the wind.
You turn the machine on by flipping (= operating) the switch on the side.
The captains flipped a coin into the air (= made it turn over in the air to see which side it landed on) to decide which side would bat first.

definitions.definition: to cook something by turning it over several times over heat
examples: I don't want to spend the rest of my life flipping burgers.

definitions.definition: to buy something, such as a house, that is not in very good condition, improve it, then sell it quickly for more money
examples: I am going to take three weeks' vacation and flip this house.
She makes money flipping old and tired furniture pieces.

definitions.definition: to make a statement in court against someone when you are both accused of a crime; to persuade someone to do this
examples: There are cases in which prosecutors have threatened to seek the death penalty unless a witness flips against a co-defendant.

definitions.definition: to change from supporting one political party to supporting a different one; to make people in a particular place do this
examples: They produced a list of the districts that are considered most likely to flip in the upcoming elections.

definitions.definition: an occasion when something turns over quickly or repeatedly

definitions.definition: to use physical strength or effort to make something move or open
examples: Move your leg up gently when you're doing this exercise, but don't force it.
If you force the zip, it'll break.
She forced her way through the crowd.

definitions.definition: to break a lock, door, window, etc. in order to allow someone to get in
examples: I forgot my key, so I had to force a window.
The police had forced open the door because nobody had answered.
The burglar forced an entry (= broke a window, door, etc. to get into the house).

definitions.definition: Forcible actions involve the use of physical power or of violence
examples: The police's forcible entry into the building has come under a lot of criticism.

definitions.definition: happening or done against someone's wishes, especially with the use of physical force
examples: There's a law to protect refugees from forcible return to countries where they face persecution.

definitions.definition: in a way that uses physical power or violence
examples: Several rioters were forcibly removed from the town square.
They have plans to seize the island forcibly before its military can develop further.

definitions.definition: in a way that happens or is done against someone's wishes, especially with the use of physical force
examples: No one is likely to be forcibly vaccinated against their will.
She was soon to be forcibly married.

definitions.definition: in a way that has a strong effect
examples: They will all argue forcibly that poor people need more money.
There is one thing that has struck me forcibly since I came to Ireland.

definitions.definition: present participle of force
to make something happen or make someone do something difficult, unpleasant, or unusual, especially by threatening or not offering the possibility of choice
examples: I really have to force myself to be nice to him.
You can't force her to make a decision.
Hospitals are being forced to close departments because of lack of money.
You could tell he was having to force back the tears (= stop himself from crying).
I didn't actually want any more dessert, but Julia forced it on me (= made me accept it).
I couldn't stay at their flat - I'd feel as if I was forcing myself on them (= making them allow me to stay).
You never tell me how you're feeling - I have to force it out of you (= make you tell me)!

definitions.definition: If plants or vegetables are forced, they are made to grow faster by artificially controlling growing conditions such as the amount of heat and light

Dad's always forgetting (to take) his pills.

definitions.definition: to not bring something with you because you did not remember it
examples: I forgot my keys.

definitions.definition: to stop thinking about someone or something
examples: He tried to forget her.
It seemed unlikely that the debt would ever be paid off so we just forgot (about) it.

definitions.definition: to act in a socially unacceptable way because you have lost control of your emotions
examples: He was so angry he forgot himself and swore loudly.

definitions.definition: present participle of forge
to make an illegal copy of something in order to deceive
examples: a forged passport
a forged signature

definitions.definition: to make or produce something, especially with some difficulty
examples: The accident forged a close bond between the two families.

definitions.definition: to suddenly and quickly move forward
examples: Just 100 metres from the finishing line Jackson forged ahead.

definitions.definition: used to say that you can forgive something because you understand it

definitions.definition: to stop blaming or being angry with someone for something that person has done, or not punish them for something
examples: I don't think she's ever quite forgiven me for getting her name wrong that time.
I've never found it easy to forgive and forget (= to behave as if something wrong had never happened).

definitions.definition: used before you ask or say something that might seem rude

definitions.definition: to say that money does not have to be paid back

definitions.definition: to make food last a long time by storing it at a very low temperature so that it becomes hard
examples: I'll freeze any food that's left over.
Most soups freeze (= can be preserved by being stored at a very low temperature) well.

definitions.definition: If an engine or lock freezes, it stops working because its parts have become stuck and can no longer move
examples: My old bicycle was so rusty that the gears had frozen.
If the lock has frozen up, try lubricating it with oil.

definitions.definition: If a person or animal that is moving freezes, it stops suddenly and becomes completely still, especially because of fear
examples: She saw someone outside the window and froze.
"Freeze (= don't move) or I'll shoot!" screamed the gunman.

definitions.definition: To freeze something such as pay or prices is to fix it at a particular level and not allow any increases
examples: The government has frozen pensions until the end of next year.

definitions.definition: to officially and legally prevent money or property from being used or moved
examples: When it was obvious the company was going bankrupt, the government ordered all their assets to be frozen.

a temporary stopping of something

definitions.definition: A friendly game or argument is one that you play or have for pleasure and in order to practise your skills, rather than playing or arguing seriously with the aim of winning
examples: We were having a friendly argument about politics.
The teams are playing a friendly match on Sunday.

definitions.definition: Friendly countries and friendly soldiers are ones who are not your enemies and who are working or fighting with you.
a game that is played for enjoyment and in order to practise, not with the aim of winning points as part of a serious competition
examples: The rugby club has a friendly next week against the Giants.

definitions.definition: used at the end of words to mean "not harmful"
examples: ozone-friendly aerosols
dolphin-friendly tuna (= fish caught without harming dolphins) .

definitions.definition: used at the end of words to mean "suitable for particular people to use"
examples: a family-friendly restaurant

definitions.definition: behaving in a pleasant, kind way towards someone
examples: a friendly face/smile
Our neighbours have always been very friendlyto/towards us.
I'm on friendly terms with my daughter's teacher.
Are you friendly with (= a friend of) Graham?

definitions.definition: a person who you know well and who you like a lot, but who is usually not a member of your family

definitions.definition: someone who is not an enemy and who you can trust

definitions.definition: someone who gives money to an arts organization or charity in order to support it

examples: I lay in bed shaking with fright.

used to show the place where someone or something starts
examples: What time does the flight from Amsterdam arrive?
The wind is coming from the north.
She sent me a postcard from Bermuda.
He took a handkerchief from his pocket.
She took her hairbrush from her handbag and began to brush her hair.
So did you really walk all the way from Smith Street?

definitions.definition: used to show the time when something starts or the time when it was made or first existed
examples: Drinks will be served from seven o'clock.
The price of petrol will rise by 5p a gallon from tomorrow.
Most of the tapestries in this room date from the 17th century.
The museum is open from 9.30am to 6.00pm Tuesday to Sunday.

definitions.definition: starting at that time and then continuing
examples: From that day on, she vowed never to trust him again.

definitions.definition: used to show the distance between two places
examples: It's about two kilometres from the airport to your hotel.
We're about a mile from home.

definitions.definition: used to show the origin of something or someone
examples: "Where are you from?" "I'm from Italy."
I wonder who this card is from.
Could I speak to someone from the sales department?
She keeps in touch with her friends from college.
What sort of reaction did you get from him?

definitions.definition: used to show the material of which something is made
examples: The desk is made from pine.
Meringues are made from sugar and egg whites.

definitions.definition: used to show the level at which a range of things begins, such as numbers or prices
examples: Prices start from £2.99.
Tickets will cost from $10 to $45.
The number of people employed by the company has risen from 25 to 200 in three years.

definitions.definition: used to show a change in the state of someone or something
examples: Things went from bad to worse.
The story was translated from Turish to English.
Since the success of her first play, she has gone from strength to strength (= her success has continued to increase).

definitions.definition: used to show the cause of something or the reason why something happens
examples: He was rushed to hospital but died from his injuries.
She made her money from investing in property.
You could tell she wasn't lying from the fear in her voice.
Wearing the correct type of clothing will reduce the risk from radiation.

definitions.definition: used to show the facts or opinions you consider before making a judgment or decision
examples: Just from looking at the clouds, I would say it's going to rain.
It's difficult to guess what they will conclude from the evidence.

definitions.definition: used to show that someone has left a place, or that something has been removed or taken away
examples: They were exiled from their homes during the war.
Her handbag was snatched from her in the street.
A refining process is used to extract usable fuel from crude oil.

definitions.definition: If you take a smaller amount from a larger amount, you reduce the larger amount by the smaller one
examples: When you subtract 3 from 16 you get 13.

definitions.definition: used to show a difference between two people or things
examples: His opinion could hardly be more different from mine.
The two sisters are so similar that it's almost impossible to tell one from the other.

definitions.definition: used to show the position of something in comparison with other things, or the point of view of someone when considering a matter or problem
examples: From the restaurant there is a beautiful view of the ocean.
She was talking from her own experience of the problem.
From our point of view, we do not see how these changes will be beneficial to the company.

definitions.definition: used to show what someone is being protected against
examples: They found shelter from the storm under a large oak tree.

definitions.definition: used to show what someone is not allowed to do or know, or what has been stopped happening
examples: He's been banned from driving for six months.
For many years, the truth was kept from the public.
The bank loan saved her company from bankruptcy.

definitions.definition: a type of soft cheese that is often produced with fruit flavours and sugar added
a long, thin leaf of a plant
examples: Ferns and palms have fronds.

definitions.definition: a long, thin leaf of a plant
examples: Ferns and palms have fronds.

definitions.definition: the part of a building, object, or person's body that faces forward or is most often seen or used
examples: The front of the museum is very impressive.
He spilled soup all down the front of his shirt.
He was lying on his front.
The actor walked out to the front of the stage.
The shop front occupies a very prominent position on the main street.

definitions.definition: the part of a vehicle that is nearest to its direction of movement
examples: Do you want to sit in the front (= next to the driver)?
If we sit near the front of the bus, we'll have a better view.

definitions.definition: the outside part or cover of a book, newspaper, or magazine
examples: There was a picture of the Trevi fountain on the front of the book.

definitions.definition: one of the first pages in a book
examples: There's an inscription in the front of the book.

definitions.definition: further forward than someone or something else
examples: The car in front suddenly stopped and I slammed on the brakes.
She started talking to the man in front of her.

definitions.definition: winning a game or competition
examples: By half time the Italians were well in front.

definitions.definition: close to the front part of something
examples: There's parking space in front of the hotel.

definitions.definition: where someone can see or hear you
examples: Please don't swear in front of the children.

definitions.definition: If you give someone an amount of money up front, you pay that person before they do something for you
examples: He wants all the money up front or he won't do the job.

definitions.definition: a particular area of activity
examples: How are things on the work front? (= Is the situation at work satisfactory?)
She's very creative on the design front (= she is very good at design).

definitions.definition: the character or qualities that a person or organization appears to have in public that are different from their real character or qualities, and whose purpose is often to deceive people or hide an illegal activity
examples: Don't be fooled by his kindness and sensitivity - it's just a front.
She presents such a cheerful front that you'd never guess she's ill.
The machinery company was a front operation for arms smuggling.
Several trading companies were set up in the early 1960s to act as fronts for money-laundering operations.

definitions.definition: land near the sea or a lake, or the part of a town near the beach that often has a wide road or path along it
examples: Let's go for a stroll along the front.
The company specializes in building lake-front property.

definitions.definition: the place where two masses of air that have different temperatures meet
examples: A cold/warm front is approaching from the west.

definitions.definition: the place where fighting takes place in a war
examples: He was a soldier on the Western front in World War I.

definitions.definition: in or at the front of something
examples: One of his front teeth is missing.
I'd like seats on the front row.
a dog's front paws

definitions.definition: If a building or area fronts (onto) a particular place, it is near it and faces it
examples: All the apartments front onto the sea.

definitions.definition: to lead an organization or group of musicians
examples: She fronts a large IT company.

definitions.definition: If a building is fronted with something, its surface is covered with it
examples: The kitchen has oak cabinets fronted with glass.

definitions.definition: a group of people sharing a political belief who perform actions in public to achieve their aims
examples: The National Front is an extremely right-wing political party in Britain.
The Animal Liberation Front has claimed responsibility for releasing the monkeys from the laboratory.

definitions.definition: the front part of a building that faces a road or river, or land near a road or river

definitions.definition: a substance that is used to provide heat or power, usually by being burned

definitions.definition: shining brightly
examples: The fulgent starlight cast hard shadows on the landscape below.

definitions.definition: (of a container or a space) holding or containing as much as possible or a lot
examples: This cup is very full so be careful with it.
My plate was already full.
I tried to get in the cinema last night but it was full.
Don't talk with your mouth full!
The shelves were full of books.
When she looked at him her eyes were full of tears.
I tried to get on the 8.45 train but it was full.
Don't fill your glass too full or you'll spill it.
The stadium was only half full.

definitions.definition: containing a lot of things or people or a lot of something
examples: This sweater is full of holes.
His essay was full of spelling errors.
I'm full of admiration for you.
You're always so full of energy.

definitions.definition: involving a lot of activities
examples: I've got a full week next week - could we postpone our meeting?
She has a very full life.

definitions.definition: to be talking or thinking a lot about something that you have enjoyed or found exciting
examples: "Did the kids enjoy their trip to the zoo?" "Oh, yes, they were full of it when they got back this afternoon."

definitions.definition: to think and act as if you are very important
examples: Since he got his new job, he's been very full of his own importance.

definitions.definition: to think that you are very important in a way that annoys other people
examples: I can't stand her - she's so full of herself.

definitions.definition: complete, whole, or containing a lot of detail
examples: Please give your full name and address.
We do not yet have full details of the story.
Few journalists have managed to convey the full horror of the situation.
Scientists have not yet determined the full impact of the oil spill.
Today's my last full day in Paris.
He unwound the rope to its full extent.
Are you a full member (= do you have all the membership rights) of the club?
Some plants need to be in full sun (= to have the sun shining on them) all the time.

definitions.definition: completely
examples: The bill must be paid in full by the end of the month.

definitions.definition: If an activity is in full flow, it is happening fast and with energy
examples: Preparations for the event are now in full flow.

definitions.definition: If an event is in full swing, it has already been happening for a period of time and there is a lot of activity
examples: The party was in full swing by the time we arrived.

definitions.definition: able to be seen by other people
examples: Andy kissed Vicki full on the lips in full view of her friends.

definitions.definition: the greatest possible
examples: James is very bright, but he doesn't make full use of his abilities.
Nobody got full marks (= all the answers right) in the spelling test.
It doesn't seem likely that we will see a return to full employment (= that all the people in the country will have a job) in the near future.

definitions.definition: having eaten so much food that you cannot eat any more
examples: No more cake for me, thanks, I'm full.

definitions.definition: having recently eaten
examples: Never go swimming on a full stomach.

definitions.definition: (of clothing) loose or containing a lot of material, or (of parts of the body) quite large and rounded
examples: a full skirt
Women often have full faces/become full in the face when they're pregnant.
Her full lips curved into a smile.

definitions.definition: used to avoid saying "fat"
examples: They advertise clothes "for the fuller figure".

definitions.definition: (of a flavour, sound, smell, etc.) strong or deep
examples: This wine has a full fruity flavour.
A cello has a fuller sound than a violin.

definitions.definition: to understand a situation completely
examples: You know full well that you're not supposed to go there without asking me!

definitions.definition: straight; directly
examples: He was kicked full in the stomach.
The intruders turned and ran as the police shone their torches full on them.

definitions.definition: as much or as well as possible
examples: She certainly lives life to the full.

definitions.definition: slightly crazy

definitions.definition: in order to be used or looked at some time in the future

examples: He showed me a photo of workers fuzing fireworks in a factory.
No projectile should be fuzed until it has been filled.

definitions.definition: a covering of short thin soft hairs, or a mass of tightly curled and often untidy hair
examples: He's got that bit of adolescent fuzz on his upper lip.
I must buy a razor today and get rid of the fuzz on my legs!

definitions.definition: the police
examples: Watch out! It's the fuzz.

definitions.definition: (of an image) having shapes that do not have clear edges, or (of a sound, especially from a television, radio, etc.) not clear, usually because of other unwanted noises making it difficult to hear
examples: Is the picture always fuzzy on your TV?
You can pick up a lot of stations on the car radio but the sound is usually sort of fuzzy.

definitions.definition: not clear
examples: The basic facts of the story are starting to emerge though the details are still fuzzy.
My head's a little fuzzy (= I cannot think clearly) this morning after all that wine last night.

definitions.definition: (of hair) in an untidy mass of tight curls
examples: Oh no, it's raining - my hair will get all fuzzy.

definitions.definition: A fuzzy surface feels like short fur
examples: the fuzzy skin of a peach

definitions.definition: in a way that is not clear to see or hear
examples: An announcement came fuzzily through the loudspeakers.

definitions.definition: in a way that is not clear to understand, especially because of not being exact or detailed enough
examples: The film asks us to forget our preconceptions about such fuzzily defined notions as "girls" or "young people".

definitions.definition: the quality of not being clear to see or hear
examples: Their videos interweave visual contrasts such as black and white versus colour and clarity versus fuzziness.

definitions.definition: the quality of being not detailed or exact enough
examples: That language is not very helpful, given the fuzziness of ideas about what "organic" means.
